era_context: Continuation of the Second Intermediate Period in Egypt; Kassite Babylon consolidating power in Mesopotamia; Shang dynasty in China; Middle Minoan period in the Aegean. This era is characterized by significant political fragmentation in Egypt, the rise of new military technologies (chariot warfare), and the emergence of Kassite Babylon as a major regional power.

events.title: Continued Hyksos rule in Egypt; possible military campaigns
events.region: Egypt (Lower Egypt / Nile Delta and possibly Upper Egypt)
events.description: During the Second Intermediate Period, the Hyksos maintained control over Lower Egypt and possibly extended influence into Upper Egypt. Archaeological evidence suggests periodic military activity and the consolidation of administrative control. The Hyksos introduced or perfected the use of the horse-drawn chariot in Egypt, which became a defining feature of New Kingdom warfare. However, specific military campaigns for this individual year are not documented in surviving sources.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Avaris and other Delta sites
Later Egyptian narratives (New Kingdom texts describing the Hyksos period)

events.certainty_note: No specific events are documented for this year. The broader context of Hyksos rule is archaeologically confirmed, but year-by-year narrative is not available.

events.title: Shang dynasty in China; oracle bone divination practices
events.description: The Shang dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) is in its mature phase. The Shang court engaged in extensive divination practices using oracle bones (scapulae and plastrons of animals, primarily oxen and tortoises), which were heated and cracked to interpret the will of ancestors and deities. These oracle bones, inscribed with questions and answers, provide some of the earliest written records in China. The Shang maintained a complex bureaucratic state with a capital at Zhengzhou and later Anyang, supported by bronze metallurgy and agricultural surplus.
key_figures: Shang kings (names uncertain for this specific year; later sources list rulers like Wu Ding, but chronology is debated)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE
Archaeological evidence from Anyang and Zhengzhou

events.certainty_note: The Shang dynasty's existence and cultural practices are well-attested archaeologically. However, the chronology of Shang rulers is highly debated, with competing chronologies differing by up to 200 years. The assignment of this year to a specific Shang reign is therefore uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for events in this year, as no specific events are documented. The broader historiographic issues noted for -1639 apply here as well: the Hyksos period is not the 'invasion' and 'oppression' described in later Egyptian sources, and Shang chronology remains contested.
historiographic_note: For 1639 BCE, the historical record is even sparser than for 1640 BCE. No specific events are documented for this year in any surviving source. The era context is known from broader archaeological and textual evidence, but year-by-year narrative is not available. This is typical for the mid-2nd millennium BCE outside of a few well-documented regions (Egypt, Mesopotamia, China). The assignment of events to this specific year is largely conventional; they could have occurred within a range of ±5–10 years.
